speaker: interviewer
text: Date, month, 9th day, 12th. Year, 1997. Survivor first name, Eva Miriam, E-V-A M-I-R-I-A-M. Freedman, the name in present, F-R-E-E-D-M-A-N. The name at birth, Mannheimer, M-A-N-N-H-E-I-M-E-R. Moderator, Viera Skakalova, V-I-E-R-A S-K-A-K-A-L-O-V-A. City, Nitra. Country, Slovakia. Language, English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Viera Skakalova. It's 12th of September of 1997. We are talking to Madam Eva Miriam Freedman, in the city Nitra in Slovakia. It's an addition to the first part of an interview, which was recorded in London in the autumn 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Madam Freedman, will you be so kind and tell us something about this place where, where we were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's quite an emotional place to be, for me, today, here. I came to Slovakia for a special reason. The two people who saved our lives, Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] were honored the Righteous Gentile in, in Bratislava, in the presence of President Michal Kovac and the Israeli ambassador to Austria, Yoel Sher. They were presented a medallion. And I was honored to be there on this occasion with my family. And I addressed Holocaust survivors. And when they heard that I am in Slovakia, they were very impressed that I turned out quite a normal, balanced human being. And they asked me to probably go back to my place, where I was in my hiding, in Nitra. And I brought my family over to Nitra on this occasion. And at the present, I'm sitting in [NON-ENGLISH] 42, where my late uncle, Miklos Fisher and Ella Fisher lived. And this is quite a special feeling for me and brings many, many happy and painful memories, mainly painful memories, of being in the part of the flat that we were hiding during the last 10, 12 months of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the period of 1944 until 1945, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this apartment, yes. But in another part, we were living in [NON-ENGLISH]. We were living since '41. And also, we had not been completely in confined hiding. But there were times we had to be in hiding. And as our father were-- and the men, my brother-- were looked at, looked by the Gestapo. All the male members of the family have going around periodically in great hiding. And we had to also go sometime in hiding, because we tried to avoid to be caught, obviously particularly my brother, who was only 16 years old at that time. And they were taking young men to the concentration camp quite young. And obviously, he moved away eventually from Slovakia to Hungary, because of the rumors at this time periodically the situation in Hungary is better. And so this Nitra is-- brings up very, very painful and sad memories for me. And being here today--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in, in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came first, I was about six years old, seven nearly. I was born in '34. And I was-- in this particular place, I was from the age of nine, 10, and probably a bit more, until the liberation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course the flat, the part where I'm sitting is completely changed. Because when my uncle and  aunt lived, it was very much like it used to be during the war. They couldn't afford to renovate it. They were struggling very, very hard. The family who lives here, Josef and Jarka [NON-ENGLISH], who kindly enough let me and my family to come into this apartment, to show for the occasion [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the apartment. So I'm most grateful to those two people. They let us come here and make this film.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us what was here before at this room?

speaker: interviewee
text: This room was completely different. One part of the room, it was divided. This was a tiny little room behind me, what was possibly a maid's room. But there was just about enough room for a bed. And the room where we are sitting now was a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now it turns out, I think a study or a television room. And this is the room I spent quite many, many, many days having my meals with my uncle and aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember some special stories from this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have got plenty of pleasant stories but mainly unpleasant stories. We were living in [NON-ENGLISH]. We come from a very, very orthodox family. But my Uncle Miklos and Ella, they were Neologs, what was kind of a reform. And it was for me a beginning of understanding of two different ways of living and thinking. My father was extremely orthodox. And he wasn't all that happy I used to come to this apartment. But on the other hand, my mother-- it was my mother's sister-- and they didn't have any children. And they were absolutely wonderful to me. They spoiled me. I had so many goodies here. And my memory is always just full of love and caring and a lot of presents. It was kind of a sanctuary. I used to love coming here, because every time I came, there were some goodies. But of course, eventually, when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the situation become worse, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things become less and less. But there was still always a special hospitality for my two uncle and aunt. And obviously, there were also family gathering, not in this room, but in the other room. And they used to play cards, what was quite unusual. Because in my household it was forbidden, or my father didn't have time-- or must be his reason. He was very religious. But in this house, there were a different kind of things happening. And they had a big social life, and many friends coming and going. But one of the rooms that we are not sitting in, it is in the other side of this building, was the room that we were hiding. And it was quite a strange way how we got in here. My uncle and my aunt were good friends of William Gavalovic, who was an accountant in the mill with my uncle, who was also an accountant and a very dear friend. Mr. Pernik, who was looking after us, he was a caretaker of the building. He had to be-- he had to know about our well-being, because he knew everything that was going on in the apartment. So he was actually put into confidence. At the beginning he got a little money. But I think he did it most of the time without money, what he did for us. And we were going up and down one day, looking for our father. There was a siren. All the Jews had to be segregated in a place. We tried to look for our father, who was working not far from the Nitra River. And he worked in a brick factory. And we couldn't go through the bridge. Because there were some Slovakian soldiers told us, don't cross the bridge. If you cross the bridge, you won't be able to come back. There was terrible panic everywhere. So we were going up and down this road. And we heard, Olga, Olga-- somebody calling out. We couldn't believe it. And my aunt-- my mother may have known they are somewhere in this building--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the name of your mother, yes? Olga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Olga Mannheimer, yes. And they called us in. And we went into the room next door. And we didn't know how long this was going to last. Nobody knew that this madness, this war is going to last and taking Jews and putting them in concentration camps. We did not know. Or we were in that room for about a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the names of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miklos Fisher was my uncle. Ella Fisher was my aunt. Olga Mannheimer, me, and my mother. My sister was Gertie Mannheimer, and I was Eva. Then my mother's other two sisters was Lilly Weiss, Seren Paskus. And Lilly had a son, Robby Weiss. This consisted eight of us, three children and five adults.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So we spent there for three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a little bit more than three days, because as they did not know the situation, what's going to happen. And then Pernik and Gavalovic thought this is not a good place to be. It's on the ground floor, and people are coming to the building. And if anybody comes to this apartment, which was supposed to be empty at the time, and, and he had to bring us food or whatever we needed. They decided that Mr.-- they will move us to another apartment. On the second floor, there was an empty flat of a secretary, who actually-- they made her to be transferred to another branch to work, so that people in the block thought that she's here. But the official in the office in the mill moved her to work somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they moved us upstairs. And then we were upstairs for most of the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then later on, as this building was occupied by the Gestapo-- When it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gestapo?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when? When it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't tell you exactly. But after two or three months-- this was the nicest block of flats at that time in Nitra. And the official of the Gestapo saw it is near the river. It is a good area. It would be nice to have some apartments. So that was a very serious situation for these people, what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mr. Pernik [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went downstairs, where every member of the block had a cellar-- basement. Within the basement there was a cellar. And he carved out a hole, about 6 foot by 4 foot. And he put outside shelves for jams and jars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind that shelf we were hiding. When there was some rumor going the Jews are hiding or the Gestapo got drunk and all kinds of stories were going on, we would dash downstairs into the cellar. And there we were for days sometimes, until things calmed down. And then we could go back again to our apartment upstairs. And we were commuting very often. It was cold. It was wintertime. It was very, very damp and very, very small. What was the worst when the Gestapo moved here, they found a very interesting and nice building and a good basement. They used to train-- gym, sports, or exercises. The soldiers would come nearly every day down, into the basement, while we were in the basement within two or three meters from the-- a few meters from the soldiers. What they gave us, big eiderdown. And if you apparently cough into the eiderdown, the echo doesn't come through. So we have constantly some covers on our mouths. We could not afford to have a cold or sneeze or clearing our throat. Because we never know if any of the soldiers are in the basement or any other people who live here, who will go down and collect some stuff from the, from the basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your feelings when the Gestapo was so close?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be honest. I don't remember any feelings. I'm able to talk about it the last few years because I had psychotherapy. I couldn't talk about this kind of feeling. My memory was blocked completely. I tried to-- I didn't-- my mind was blocked up until the age of 10. I tried not to remember anything what happened for many, many years. But I realize as I'm working with people myself, counseling, I realize it's the only way I can cure myself, to go through the process of therapy. You can't help anybody else. And then many, many painful feelings came through. I was going for six months just crying. And terrible depressions I had. I was quite ill for a very long time. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] blocked feelings came to the surface. But to-- if I remember the feeling of that time, I wouldn't say I remembered anything. I just remembered that I was an outcast. I was deprived of liberty. I had no friends. I had no schooling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Undernourished, no clothes, no books, nothing. I was just-- I would say nothing. I would consider myself to be worse than a dog. We were nothing. You know, when you actually-- your voice can not be heard and you're not able to speak, you can't really say that you're something. You have to make a statement in your life to impress people that you have got a voice and a mind and a feeling. That took many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the basement where we were hiding during the Second World War. And I hardly can believe when I look at it now what it looks like. This was the entry of the basement. And I'd like to show you inside the basement. It was empty, this part. There is at the moment some furniture here. But this was nothing in it. And Mr. Pernik realized that he has to find another hiding place for us, in case some dangerous situation arises. Within the basement he carved out a hole. Because he realized that the basement was exposed with wooden slats here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he carved a hole there. And he put eight members of our family inside that hole. And he put in front of it some shelves. Now, it was quite customary in continental cellars and basements to keep your jam and your pickles and other sorts of household goods that you don't use in the basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in front of the entry of the hole that we were hiding eight people. It's quite difficult for me to believe, because my three aunties and my mother were very, very, tall, beautiful ladies. They were 5 foot 8, 5 foot 10. My uncle was a good size of a man. And we were three children. And look at it, at this hole, what is approximately 2 and 1/2 meters by 1 and 1/2 meters, that we managed to actually get in. And not only get in, but live there for days upon days, without fresh air, no movement whatsoever [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and shivering, because winter was coming near. And it's just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you have any possibility to move and go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we couldn't-- we were only walking out when we utterly were safe, maybe at nighttime or a particular time when we had to use a toilet, but what was obviously not a proper toilet. We could not move when we were actually inside. We were huddling together, one to-- next to each other. We had-- no, we did not move at all. It was a very, very confined space.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was brought up to us at nighttime or sometime very, very early in the morning, when most of the people in the building were asleep. And we would come out from this hole to this part, and sitting here and eating it. And I can't recollect at all washing at all. And as soon as we finished our food Mr. Pernik brought us, we went back to the place. In front of it, he put some shelves. And he put all the necessary jars and so on. And we had some-- a little water in a bucket, so if we had to drink some water, and-- no, we were just utterly like a sardine, lying there, doing nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you eat daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recollect only eating once a day. But I believe we had a little food in the morning. Because you know what, I was completely blocked as a child. I didn't want to remember that period. I do remember in the evening we used to come out here, and it was dark, no lights. Probably candles or so, but no light at all. We had to be very, very careful. And we were eating here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who prepared this meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was prepared by the caretaker of the building, who was in charge of us. Because he was the only one who actually could go to different parts of the building or places without being suspicious. And this was his basement as well. So he could come down to his basement even five times a day. And secondly, he was in charge of the central heating system and the washroom. So he had good reason to come down into the basement. This is-- I think, on the, on the right are the stairs that we must have come down. It's plastered. And we came in here. And I remember that I was sitting on the right-hand side. Now, this part was not in existence. But if you look further down, there's an old pipe that was going all along here. And I was squeezed into this little corner, because I was the youngest member of the family. And they thought I don't need much space. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then my mother, my sister, my aunt, all were sitting next to each other. And they were giving me an eiderdown, a quilt. Because we were told you're not allowed to sneeze. You're not allowed to cough or to cry or to talk. And all I remember is move, move, move! Because I was so young, they thought I can go further. And I couldn't go any further than this place. And this pipe was hurting me so much. I was lying on this pipe. This is a new modern pipe now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were here like sardines, squeezed. And in front of here, there was the shelves. And the shelves were open, but we could actually-- because we needed some breathing. There was no air coming from anywhere. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed here for days.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is the basement that the German soldiers used to come and train daily, physical exercises. I can't tell you exactly how many of them. But there must have been quite a number of them, because we could hear them. We were hiding behind this wall. It was about-- it was 6 centimeters, not a very thick wall. Behind this wall-- and obviously, we could hear, because the echo of the basement is very loud-- we could hear them when they were doing all kinds of movements. But occasionally, they stopped. Now we, in that particular basement, we could not say a sound. Because if any of us would have coughed or any kind of sound, sneeze, they could probably hear it. So we were all, all notified from the minute-- or we heard them as soon as they walked in to the basement. We had to be there in utter silence, sometimes up to a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans come down also in another time daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they did, and they also were suspecting. They-- I think they were suspicious, not from us, but there may have been some informers. Because the very incident that we could hear one or two people walking. We did not know if they were German soldiers or the local people, because there are the cellars here and-- in the basement. But we could hear-- I don't know if they came to clean the place up or to air-- but we did hear some soldiers walking because they spoke German.
